6. Explain that filtered water still requires boiling or chemical treatment before drinking, reinforcing multi-step purification as a survival principle.
7. Have members disassemble, clean, and properly dispose of materials, emphasizing leave-no-trace ethics.
materials: 16 x coffee filters or cotton cloth squares
2 buckets of muddy water (soil mixed in)
1 x scissors or craft knife (leader use)

objective: Members will assemble a simple solar oven from a cardboard box and foil, understanding how solar energy can be harnessed for cooking without fuel.
instructions:
1. Begin with a 3-minute briefing on solar cooking — explain how Lebanon's abundant sunshine makes solar ovens a practical, zero-emission cooking method.
2. Distribute pre-cut cardboard boxes and guide members to line the interior flaps with aluminum foil, shiny side out, using glue sticks to secure the foil flat.
3. Show members how to angle the reflective flaps to direct sunlight toward the center cooking area, and place a sheet of clear plastic wrap or a glass pane over the box opening to trap heat.
4. Place a small dark-colored metal tray with a thin layer of pre-made ka'ak dough (prepared ahead) inside each oven and position them in direct sunlight.
5. Use a thermometer to measure the temperature inside the oven every 3 minutes, recording observations in notebooks to track heat buildup.
6. While waiting, lead a group discussion on how solar cooking reduces reliance on wood burning, which contributes to deforestation in Lebanese cedar regions.
7. Conclude by comparing solar oven temperatures to conventional cooking temperatures, and distribute any partially cooked ka'ak as a reward, reinforcing the practical value of the skill.
materials: 8 x sheets of clear plastic wrap (30x30 cm)
1 x batch of pre-made ka'ak dough (enough for 8 small portions)

objective: Members will correctly identify at least five wild plants native to Lebanon, distinguishing edible from toxic species using visual identification cards.
instructions:
1. Distribute laminated plant identification cards featuring 10 Lebanese wild plants (e.g., za'atar, sumac, wild sage, hemlock, oleander, chamomile, wild thyme, caper, thistle, euphorbia) — 5 edible/medicinal and 5 toxic.
2. Explain the universal rules of plant safety: never eat any wild plant unless 100% certain of identification, never touch unfamiliar plants with bare hands, and always check multiple identifying features (leaf shape, smell, stem, habitat).
3. Set up 8 labeled plant sample stations around the outdoor area, each containing a real or high-quality printed specimen of one plant. Members rotate through stations in pairs.
4. At each station, members examine the plant, consult their identification cards, and record their identification and whether they classify it as safe or toxic in their notebooks.
5. After all pairs have visited every station, gather the group and review each specimen together, correcting any misidentifications and explaining the key distinguishing features.
6. Quiz members verbally: hold up a card and call on individuals to name the plant, one edible use or one danger, and one identifying feature.
7. Close with a safety reminder: in a wilderness emergency, knowledge of local flora can be life-saving, but caution always takes priority over hunger.
materials: 16 x laminated plant identification cards (set of 10 plants, 2 copies per pair)
8 x real or printed plant specimens mounted at stations
8 x disposable gloves (for handling unknown specimens)
8 x station marker flags or number signs

objective: Members will successfully cook a simple food item using only natural materials and a campfire, developing primitive outdoor cooking techniques.
instructions:
1. Open with a 2-minute introduction on primitive cooking traditions in Lebanon's mountain communities — explain that scouts must be capable of preparing food in the field without modern equipment.
2. Demonstrate the two techniques to be practiced: (a) ember cooking — wrapping a small potato in wet leaves and placing it directly in hot embers, and (b) stick roasting — skewering a piece of halloumi cheese on a green (non-toxic) stick and holding it over the flame.
3. Divide members into pairs and assign each pair one technique. Ensure the campfire or charcoal brazier is already lit and at a safe cooking temperature before the activity begins.
4. Guide members through selecting appropriate green sticks (for skewering) or large wet leaves (for wrapping), emphasizing that dry sticks will burn and that leaf selection matters.
5. Supervise closely as each pair prepares and cooks their food item, coaching on fire distance, rotation technique, and recognizing when food is cooked through.
6. Have pairs swap techniques if time permits, ensuring everyone experiences both methods.
7. Gather for a 3-minute debrief: discuss food safety in primitive cooking, how to judge doneness without utensils, and when these skills would be used in a real wilderness scenario.
materials: 1 x established campfire or charcoal brazier (pre-lit by leader)
16 x large fresh leaves (fig or grape leaves — for wrapping)
8 x green non-toxic sticks (approximately 50 cm long)
1 x fire poker/tongs for leader use

activity_name: Scout Pulse and Recovery Rate Station
objective: Members will accurately measure resting and active pulse rates, calculate recovery time, and understand what these readings indicate about cardiovascular health.
instructions:
1. Explain the purpose of pulse monitoring: scouts must be able to assess their own and others' physical condition in the field, where medical equipment is unavailable.
2. Teach the two-finger technique for locating the radial pulse (wrist) and the carotid pulse (neck), demonstrating correct finger placement and light pressure on a volunteer.
3. Have all members sit quietly for 2 minutes, then take their resting pulse by counting beats for 30 seconds and multiplying by 2. Record in notebooks.
4. Lead the group through 90 seconds of jumping jacks to elevate heart rate, then immediately have members retake their pulse using the same 30-second count method and record the result.
5. Start a stopwatch and have members retake their pulse every minute until it returns within 10 beats of their resting rate — recording the time taken as their 'recovery time.'
6. Display a reference chart showing healthy resting pulse ranges by age group (60–100 bpm for teens) and explain what abnormally high resting rates or slow recovery times might indicate.
7. Pair members to practice on each other, with one measuring the pulse and the other recording, then swap roles — reinforcing that this is a skill used in first aid assessments.
materials: 1 x large printed pulse reference chart (posted on wall)
1 x whiteboard and marker (for group data recording)

objective: Members will construct a small demonstration compost layer and correctly classify organic waste into compostable and non-compostable categories, applying sustainability principles to daily life.
instructions:
1. Begin with a 3-minute discussion on Lebanon's waste crisis — highlight that organic waste makes up over 50% of landfill content and that composting at home and in scout camps can significantly reduce this burden.
2. Present two sorting trays and a mixed collection of 20 sample items (vegetable peels, orange rinds, bread, plastic wrap, meat scraps, eggshells, coffee grounds, glossy paper, grass clippings, glass). Members take turns placing each item into the correct 'compost' or 'non-compost' tray and explaining their choice.
3. Introduce the 'green and brown' layering concept: green materials (nitrogen-rich: vegetable waste, grass) and brown materials (carbon-rich: dry leaves, cardboard) must alternate to create effective compost.
4. Distribute small containers (1 per pair) and guide members to layer their mini-compost demonstration: a thin base of soil, a layer of brown material (torn cardboard), a layer of green material (vegetable scraps provided), and a thin covering of soil.
5. Add a small amount of water to each container to demonstrate correct moisture level — damp but not saturated — and place a ventilated lid on top.
6. Explain ongoing maintenance: turning the pile, moisture management, and what a healthy compost smells like versus a problematic one, referencing how scouts can maintain a compost system at a camp.
7. Close with members committing to one composting action they can take at home this week, sharing it with a partner.
materials: 20 x assorted sample waste items (mix of organic and non-organic)
8 x small plastic containers with ventilated lids (1 per pair)
1 x bag of dry leaves or torn cardboard (brown material)
500 g vegetable scraps (pre-collected: peels, coffee grounds, eggshells)
1 x printed green/brown material reference chart

activity_name: Wilderness Navigation by Sun and Shadow
objective: Members will determine cardinal directions using the shadow-stick method and the watch method, applying these techniques to orient themselves without a compass.
instructions:
1. Introduce the session by explaining that in Lebanon's mountain ranges and rural terrain, electronic devices may fail — scouts must be able to navigate using the sun, which is reliable in Lebanon's predominantly sunny climate.
2. Demonstrate the shadow-stick method: push a 50 cm stick vertically into the ground, mark the tip of its shadow with a stone, wait 10–15 minutes, then mark the new shadow tip with a second stone. Draw a line between the two stones — this east-west line is drawn from the first stone (west) to the second stone (east). Draw a perpendicular line to find north-south.
3. Have each member or pair set up their own shadow stick simultaneously to practice the technique, using the 10-minute wait time for the next instruction step.
4. While waiting, teach the analog watch method: point the hour hand at the sun; the point halfway between the hour hand and the 12 o'clock mark indicates south (in the northern hemisphere — applicable to Lebanon).
5. After the shadow-stick wait time elapses, members complete their orientation line and use both methods to determine north, then compare their results with a compass provided by the leader.
6. Pose a practical scenario: 'You are 3 km north of a village in the Chouf mountains with no compass. Using what you just learned, describe in two sentences how you would navigate south toward the village.' Members write responses in notebooks.
7. Review answers as a group, highlighting the most accurate and practical responses, and summarize key memory triggers for both navigation methods.
materials: 8 x analog watches (or printed watch face cards)
1 x measuring tape (for setting up sticks at proper distance apart)

objective: Members will correctly apply a makeshift splint to a simulated arm fracture using natural and available materials, and practice a safe two-person carrying technique for a non-ambulatory casualty.
instructions:
1. Open with a realistic scenario: a scout has fallen on a rocky trail in the Qadisha Valley and is suspected of having a broken forearm — no ambulance can reach the location for 45 minutes. Members must stabilize and move the casualty.
2. Demonstrate proper splint application: select two rigid sticks slightly longer than the forearm, pad them with folded cloth or a bandana, place one on each side of the arm, and secure with three evenly spaced triangular bandages — tied firmly but not so tightly as to cut circulation.
3. Teach members to check circulation after splinting using the 3 Cs: Color (skin should not be blue or white), Capillary refill (press nail bed — color should return within 2 seconds), and Complaints (ask the casualty about numbness or tingling).
4. Divide into groups of three: one casualty, one primary aider applying the splint, one observer checking technique. Rotate roles so each member practices splint application.
5. Demonstrate the two-person seat carry: two carriers face each other and grip their own opposite wrist, then grip the other carrier's wrist to form a 'seat' — the casualty sits and places arms around the carriers' shoulders. Practice this with the casualty's arm kept stationary in the splint.
6. Each group of three practices the seat carry for 5 meters, focusing on keeping the casualty's splinted arm immobilized and maintaining a stable carry pace.
7. Debrief as a full group: ask members when NOT to move a casualty (suspected spinal injury), reinforcing that movement decisions require situational judgment, and close with three key splinting rules to memorize.
materials: 16 x straight sticks (30–35 cm long, splint-thickness)
8 x folded cloth pads or bandanas
1 x printed 3 Cs circulation check card (posted on wall)
1 x first aid reference poster (fracture management)
1 x roll of medical tape (for demonstration)

objective: Members will be able to explain the meaning of each clause of the Scout Promise and connect it to their daily lives in Lebanon.
instructions:
1. Gather members in a semicircle and write the Scout Promise on a whiteboard or flip chart.
2. Read the Promise aloud together as a group, slowly and clearly.
3. Break the Promise into three distinct clauses and assign each clause to a small group of 2-3 scouts.
4. Ask each group to spend 2 minutes discussing what their clause means in a real-life Lebanese context — at school, at home, or in their neighbourhood.
5. Invite a spokesperson from each group to share their group's interpretation with everyone.
6. The leader summarises the key ideas, highlighting how the Promise links to the Scout Law and to Lebanese civic values.
7. Close by having every member recite the Promise together standing at attention.
materials: 1 printed copy of the Scout Promise per member (20 copies)

objective: Members will distinguish between physical courage and moral courage and identify at least one situation where they can practise moral courage in their community.
instructions:
1. Begin with a brief show of hands: ask who thinks courage means only being brave in dangerous physical situations.
2. Introduce the concept of moral courage through a short 3-minute talk, defining it as the ability to do what is right even when it is difficult or unpopular.
3. Present three scenario cards — one set in a Lebanese school, one in a neighbourhood, and one within a scout patrol — where a young person must choose between the easy wrong and the difficult right.
4. Read each scenario aloud and invite members to vote silently (thumbs up/down) on what the character should do, then ask two or three volunteers to explain their reasoning.
5. Draw a two-column comparison on the board: 'Physical Courage' vs 'Moral Courage', filling it in with examples from member responses.
6. Share a brief real story of a Lebanese figure (e.g., a civil war-era aid worker or a modern environmental activist) who demonstrated moral courage.
7. Ask each member to write one sentence on a sticky note describing a moment this week where they could show moral courage, then stick it anonymously on the board.

objective: Members will articulate why honesty is a foundational Scout Law principle and describe the consequences of dishonesty within a patrol and community.
instructions:
1. Open with a brief imaginary scenario: the patrol is navigating a mountain trail in the Chouf and one scout gives the wrong compass bearing to save face — ask members what might happen next.
2. Let 3-4 members respond freely, then guide responses toward safety risks and broken trust.
3. Present the Scout Law clause on honesty on the board, reading it aloud together.
4. Explain the concept of honesty in three dimensions: honesty with others, honesty with oneself, and honesty in action (not just words).
5. Share a short anecdote about a scouting situation where a small dishonesty caused a larger problem, keeping the story relatable to Lebanese youth.
6. Facilitate a 3-minute pair discussion: 'Tell your partner about a time when being honest was hard but important.'
7. Bring the group back together and collect 2-3 shared reflections, then close by linking honesty back to the Scout Law and the trust that holds a patrol together.

activity_name: Values We Carry in Our Bag
objective: Members will identify three core scouting values they personally hold and explain how these values guide their decisions.
instructions:
1. Place a real or symbolic backpack at the front of the room and explain that today the group will think about what 'invisible things' scouts carry with them wherever they go.
2. Ask members to call out values they think a scout should carry — write each on a paper label and dramatically place it inside the bag.
3. Once 8-10 values have been collected, pull out each label one by one and give a one-sentence explanation of why that value matters in scouting and in Lebanese society.
4. Distribute a small card to each member and ask them to write their personal top three values from the bag.
5. Pair members up and give them 2 minutes to explain to their partner why they chose those three values.
6. Lead a brief group reflection by asking: 'What happens when a scout leaves one of these values behind?' Take 3-4 responses.
7. Close by challenging each member to consciously 'pack' one value every morning this week and notice how it affects their choices.
materials: 1 small index card per member (25 cards)

objective: Members will apply an ethical decision-making framework to a real-world dilemma relevant to Lebanese youth and evaluate their choice against Scout Law principles.
instructions:
1. Introduce the session by asking: 'What is the difference between something that is legal and something that is ethical?' Allow 2-3 brief responses.
2. Draw a compass on the board with four directions labelled: Is it Legal? Is it Fair? How does it make me feel? How would it look to others? Explain this as the 'Ethics Compass'.
3. Present a dilemma relevant to Lebanese youth — for example: 'Your patrol leader asks you to cover up a mistake in the scout report to avoid embarrassing the troop at the district review. What do you do?'
4. Walk members through the dilemma using each compass direction, asking for their input at each point.
5. Invite a volunteer to come to the board and 'use the compass' on a second dilemma you present — a situation involving cheating on a community service log.
6. Discuss as a group which Scout Law principles were engaged in each dilemma.
7. Wrap up by giving each member a small printed compass card to keep as a reminder of the framework for future decisions.
materials: 1 printed Ethics Compass card per member (25 cards)
1 printed dilemma scenario sheet for the leader

activity_name: Pillars of the Scout Law Gallery
objective: Members will demonstrate their understanding of all Scout Law points by correctly matching each law to a real-life example and explaining the reasoning behind each match.
instructions:
1. Before the meeting, prepare a set of law cards (each card has one Scout Law point written on it) and a separate set of example cards (each card describes a real-life situation).
2. Spread the law cards across a table or pin them in a row on the wall and read through each one briefly as a group.
3. Shuffle the example cards and distribute two cards randomly to each member or pair.
4. Ask members to walk to the wall and place their example card beneath the Scout Law point they think it best illustrates, explaining their reasoning to the group as they do.
5. Once all cards are placed, review each pairing as a group — validate correct matches and gently redirect misplacements with open questions rather than direct correction.
6. Highlight the three or four law points most relevant to Lebanese community life (e.g., helpfulness, brotherhood/sisterhood, care for the environment) and discuss how they are lived out in Lebanese culture.
7. Close by asking members: 'Which Scout Law do you find hardest to keep — and why?' Take 3-4 honest responses to normalise the challenge of living by values.
materials: 10 Scout Law point cards (printed and laminated)
1 roll of masking tape for wall display
1 printed Scout Law reference sheet for the leader

objective: Members will recognise that silence, omission, and passive acceptance can be forms of dishonesty and will commit to at least one specific action that reflects active honesty.
instructions:
1. Open with a provocative question written on the board: 'Can you be dishonest without saying a single word?' Give members 30 seconds to think silently before taking responses.
2. Introduce the concept of 'dishonesty by omission' — choosing not to speak up when a wrong is happening — and give two brief relatable examples from school or scout life.
3. Present a scenario: a patrol member takes credit for another scout's work at a district ceremony, and the real contributor says nothing. Ask: 'Who is being dishonest here?'
4. Facilitate a structured debate: divide the room into two sides — one arguing the silent scout is dishonest, the other arguing they are not. Give each side 90 seconds to prepare one argument.
5. After both sides speak, guide the group to a nuanced conclusion: honesty as a Scout Law principle requires both truthful speech and truthful action, including speaking up.
6. Connect this to the concept of courage: sometimes honesty requires courage, and sometimes courage is the only path to honesty.
7. Close with a personal commitment: ask each member to say aloud or write one sentence beginning with 'This week I will be honest by…' and collect responses to display on the patrol noticeboard.
materials: 1 printed scenario card for the leader

activity_name: Roots and Branches: Values from Our Community
objective: Members will connect scouting values to Lebanese cultural and community traditions, identifying at least two values that appear in both scouting and their own heritage.
instructions:
1. Draw a large tree on the board with visible roots and branches. Label the roots 'Our Community Values' and the branches 'Scout Values'.
2. Ask members to call out values they have learned from family, school, or community life in Lebanon — write each on a root of the tree (e.g., hospitality, respect for elders, solidarity).
3. Next, ask members to recall the Scout Law values they know — write these on the branches.
4. Together as a group, look for connections: draw lines linking roots to branches where a community value and a scout value overlap or reinforce each other.
5. Discuss: 'How does being a Lebanese scout give you a special responsibility to carry these shared values forward?'
6. Highlight any values that appear strongly in the Lebanese context but may need extra attention in scout life (e.g., inclusivity across religious communities, environmental stewardship of the cedar forests).
7. Photograph the completed tree diagram and share it with the patrol as a visual reminder; alternatively, display it in the meeting room.
materials: 1 large flip chart sheet or poster paper
1 roll of masking tape for display

activity_name: The Brave Decision: A Leader's Dilemma
objective: Members will analyse a leadership scenario requiring moral courage and articulate the scouting values that should guide a leader's decision under pressure.
instructions:
1. Introduce the session by stating: 'Leadership is easy when everything goes well. True character shows when things go wrong.' Ask members if they agree or disagree and why.
2. Present a detailed leadership dilemma: a patrol leader discovers that two senior scouts have been falsifying community service hours to win a troop award. The patrol leader is their friend. What should they do?
3. Ask members to write down — privately — what they would do if they were the patrol leader. Give 1 minute for this.
4. Open a structured discussion using three guiding questions: What does the Scout Law say? What are the short-term and long-term consequences of each choice? What would a courageous leader do?
5. Invite 3-4 members to share their written response and reasoning — ensure responses are heard respectfully without immediate judgement.
6. Introduce the concept of 'the price of courage' — that doing the right thing sometimes costs friendship, popularity, or comfort — and link this explicitly to the scout value of courage.
7. Close by asking: 'What support systems exist in scouting to help leaders make brave decisions?' Guide responses toward patrol culture, scout leadership, and the Scout Law itself.
materials: 1 printed dilemma scenario (1 per member, 25 copies)

activity_name: Mirror of Character: Self-Assessment and the Scout Law
objective: Members will honestly evaluate their own adherence to three Scout Law principles over the past month and set one measurable personal improvement goal.
instructions:
1. Begin by explaining that great scouts, like great athletes, regularly review their own performance — not to feel bad about mistakes, but to grow intentionally.
2. Distribute the self-assessment worksheet, which lists five Scout Law principles with a simple 1-5 rating scale (1 = I rarely live this, 5 = I consistently live this) and a 'why' reflection space.
3. Allow 5 minutes of silent, individual reflection for members to complete the worksheet honestly — emphasise that no one will see their scores unless they choose to share.
4. Ask members to identify the one principle they rated themselves lowest on and write one specific reason why they find it difficult in their current Lebanese daily life context.
5. Invite 3-4 volunteers to share their reflection — model vulnerability by sharing an example from your own experience as a leader first.
6. Facilitate a brief group discussion: 'What makes it hard to live scout values consistently — in school, at home, in the community?' Guide responses without judging.
7. Ask each member to write one SMART goal (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) at the bottom of their worksheet: 'By next meeting, I will improve my [chosen law principle] by doing [specific action].' Collect worksheets to review privately and return with encouraging written feedback.
materials: 1 printed self-assessment worksheet per member (25 copies)
1 whiteboard for displaying the Scout Law (reference)

activity_name: The Rights and Duties of a Lebanese Citizen
objective: Members will be able to identify at least three constitutional rights and three civic duties of Lebanese citizens and explain how these connect to Scout Law.
instructions:
1. Begin by asking members what they think it means to be a 'good citizen' — collect 3–4 responses on a whiteboard or flip chart.
2. Present a short illustrated talk (using a poster or projected slides) covering Lebanon's constitutional rights: freedom of expression, right to education, right to vote, and equality before the law.
3. Shift focus to duties: paying taxes, military service eligibility, respecting the law, and participating in elections — discuss each briefly with a local Lebanese example.
4. Connect each civic duty to a specific point of the Scout Law (e.g., 'A Scout is loyal' links to respecting the state and community).
5. Pose a scenario: 'You see a neighbor dumping waste in the street — what are your rights and duties as a citizen in this moment?' Allow 2–3 members to respond.
6. Close with a one-minute reflection: ask each member to silently choose one civic duty they will practice before the next meeting.
materials: 1 printed or hand-drawn poster illustrating Lebanese citizen rights and duties
1 copy of simplified Lebanese constitutional excerpts (printed, 1 per leader)

activity_name: Voting Voices: How Democracy Works in Lebanon
objective: Members will understand the basic structure of Lebanon's parliamentary electoral system and recognize the importance of informed, active participation in civic life.
instructions:
1. Open with a quick show of hands: 'Who has seen a Lebanese election campaign? Who knows how many seats are in the Lebanese parliament?' Use responses to gauge prior knowledge.
2. Deliver a 10-minute structured talk covering: the confessional system in brief, how parliamentary seats are distributed, the role of a Member of Parliament, and how citizens register and vote.
3. Use a hand-drawn diagram on the board to illustrate the flow from voter registration → casting a ballot → counting → forming a government.
4. Discuss the importance of youth voices: share a fact about the 2022 Lebanese elections and the role first-time young voters played.
5. Facilitate a 3-minute structured discussion: 'What challenges might prevent a young Lebanese person from engaging civically, and how can Scouts help address them?'
6. Summarize by linking active citizenship to the Scout motto 'Be Prepared' — being prepared means being an informed citizen.
materials: 1 printed fact sheet on Lebanese parliamentary structure (1 per leader)
1 printed infographic or hand-drawn chart showing electoral process

activity_name: Hands That Build: The Culture of Volunteering in Lebanon
objective: Members will articulate the value of community volunteering and identify at least two concrete service opportunities available to Lebanese Scouts in their local area.
instructions:
1. Start with a brief story (2 minutes): share a real or realistic account of a Lebanese Scout troop that helped rebuild a community space after the 2020 Beirut port explosion — emphasize teamwork and impact.
2. Define 'community service' versus 'charity' — explain that service is about sustainable involvement and shared dignity, not just giving.
3. Present a visual list (on board or poster) of five categories of community service relevant to Lebanon: elderly care, environmental clean-up, tutoring underprivileged children, blood donation drives, and disaster relief support.
4. Discuss how Lebanese Scout troops coordinate with organizations like the Lebanese Red Cross, municipalities, and local NGOs — explain the importance of partnerships.
5. Ask members to brainstorm in pairs for 2 minutes: one community problem in their neighborhood and one way Scouts could address it.
6. Take 2–3 pair responses and record them on the board, then challenge the group to pick one idea to develop into a future service project.
materials: 1 printed or drawn visual list of community service categories
Sticky notes (1 pack of at least 20)

activity_name: Lebanon's Green Crisis: Understanding Environmental Threats
objective: Members will identify the three main environmental threats facing Lebanon — deforestation, water pollution, and solid waste mismanagement — and describe one Scout-level action to address each.
instructions:
1. Display or draw a simple map of Lebanon and mark three environmental hotspots: the Litani River basin (water pollution), a coastal area affected by waste (e.g., Dora beach), and a deforested hillside in the north.
2. Spend 4 minutes on each threat: deforestation (link to cedar tree symbolism and Lebanon's national identity), water pollution (household and industrial causes), and solid waste (the 2015 garbage crisis as a case study).
3. For each threat, present one proven solution that has been implemented in Lebanon or regionally — e.g., reforestation campaigns by NGOs like Friends of Nature Lebanon.
4. Connect the environment theme to the Scout principle of 'leaving a place better than you found it' — ask members how this applies beyond the campsite.
5. Present a 'Scout Environmental Pledge' card and read it together aloud as a group.
6. Close with a challenge: ask members to conduct a 'home environmental audit' before the next meeting — checking water usage, waste sorting, and energy habits.
materials: 1 printed or hand-drawn map of Lebanon (A3 size)
1 'Scout Environmental Pledge' card per member (printed or handwritten, 20 copies)
1 poster or printed images showing Lebanon's environmental issues

activity_name: Water Is Life: Conservation Habits for Lebanese Scouts
objective: Members will understand Lebanon's water scarcity challenges and commit to at least two water-saving habits they can implement at home and during Scout activities.
instructions:
1. Open with a striking statistic: 'Lebanon has the highest water stress index in the Arab region despite receiving significant rainfall — why?' Invite 1–2 guesses from the group.
2. Explain the water cycle in Lebanon briefly: rainfall, aquifer recharge, river systems (Litani, Nahr Ibrahim), and the mismatch between supply and infrastructure.
3. Describe key causes of Lebanon's water crisis: aging pipes (up to 40% leakage), unregulated wells, pollution of springs, and climate change affecting snowpack on the mountains.
4. Shift to solutions — present 6 practical water-saving tips with Lebanese household context: fixing dripping taps, using buckets instead of hoses for car washing, reducing shower time, reusing cooking water for plants, collecting rainwater where possible, and reporting water waste.
5. Conduct a quick interactive check: read out a scenario (e.g., 'leaving the tap running while brushing teeth') and ask members to call out the water wasted per minute.
6. End with a group commitment: each member states one water habit they will change this week, recorded on the board.
materials: 1 printed water statistics fact sheet for the leader (1 copy)
1 empty 1-liter water bottle (as a visual prop)

activity_name: Strong Mind, Strong Scout: Introduction to Mental Health Awareness
objective: Members will be able to define mental health in accessible terms, recognize two common signs of emotional distress, and identify one trusted person or resource they can turn to for support.
instructions:
1. Begin by normalizing the topic: say 'Mental health is part of our overall health — just like physical fitness, we need to take care of our minds.' Ask: 'What do you do when you feel overwhelmed?'
2. Define mental health simply: it is how we think, feel, and behave — and it changes depending on our circumstances, stress, and support system.
3. Explain that Lebanon's ongoing crises (economic hardship, displacement, political instability) create real mental health pressures — validate that many young people feel anxiety, sadness, or confusion, and that this is normal.
4. Present four common signs that someone may need emotional support: withdrawal from friends, loss of appetite or sleep, persistent sadness, or sudden changes in behavior — use illustrated cards or drawings.
5. Introduce the concept of 'mental health first aid' for Scouts: listening without judgment, not giving unsolicited advice, asking 'Are you okay?' sincerely, and connecting a peer to a trusted adult.
6. Share one available resource in Lebanon (e.g., Embrace Lebanon hotline: 1564) and encourage members to memorize it. Close with a breathing exercise: 4 counts in, hold 4, out 4.
materials: 4 illustrated cards showing signs of emotional distress (hand-drawn or printed)
1 handout per member listing Embrace Lebanon hotline and 3 self-care tips (20 copies)

activity_name: Nutrition Under Pressure: Eating Well in Challenging Times
objective: Members will identify the key elements of a balanced diet using affordable Lebanese foods and understand how proper nutrition supports physical and mental performance in Scouts.
instructions:
1. Start with a short poll: ask members to describe what they ate for breakfast — note common foods on the board and assess nutritional variety without judgment.
2. Present the five food groups using a hand-drawn food pyramid adapted to Lebanese cuisine: grains (khobz, burghul), proteins (hummus, eggs, labneh, legumes), vegetables (zaatar, tomatoes, spinach), fruits (seasonal Lebanese produce), and fats (olive oil, nuts).
3. Explain the role of each food group in a Scout's physical performance — energy from carbs, muscle repair from protein, immunity from vitamins, and hydration from fruits and water.
4. Address Lebanon's food security reality: many families face economic constraints — highlight affordable, nutritious Lebanese staples such as lentils, beans, eggs, and seasonal vegetables that provide maximum nutrition at low cost.
5. Discuss the link between nutrition and mental clarity: explain how skipping meals or eating excessive processed food affects concentration and mood during Scout meetings and school.
6. Close with a 'My Healthy Scout Meal' challenge: each member designs a nutritious one-day meal plan using Lebanese foods and shares one item with the group.
materials: 1 hand-drawn or printed Lebanese food pyramid poster (A2 size)
1 blank meal planning worksheet per member (20 copies, simple grid format)

activity_name: Bouncing Back: Building Resilience Through Scout Stories
objective: Members will define resilience, identify the four pillars that build it (connection, mindset, self-care, purpose), and reflect on a personal experience where they demonstrated resilience.
instructions:
1. Open with a question: 'Think of a time when something went wrong — a failed exam, a disappointment, a difficult moment. What helped you get through it?' Take 2–3 short responses.
2. Define resilience: 'The ability to adapt, recover, and grow stronger after challenges — it is not the absence of difficulty but the presence of strength to move forward.'
3. Present the four pillars of resilience using a drawn diagram on the board: (1) Connection — having people you trust; (2) Mindset — believing challenges can be overcome; (3) Self-Care — physical and emotional maintenance; (4) Purpose — knowing why you keep going.
4. Share two brief narrative examples: one from Lebanese history (e.g., communities rebuilding after wars or the 2020 Beirut explosion) and one from a fictional Scout who overcame a difficult camping challenge — emphasize how each pillar appeared in both stories.
5. Link resilience to Scouting: explain how every hike, camping trip, and challenge in Scouting is a deliberate training ground for resilience-building in real life.
6. Ask each member to write (on a small card) one personal value or connection that helps them be resilient — collect and place in a 'Resilience Jar' as a symbolic group keepsake to revisit later in the year.
materials: 1 small blank index card per member (20 cards)
1 glass jar or decorated tin labeled 'Resilience Jar'

objective: Members will reflect on the qualities of a lone leader who perseveres through hardship, drawing parallels to Lebanon's cedar tree as a national symbol of strength.
instructions:
1. Gather members in a close circle, seated on the ground or on chairs.
2. Begin by asking members: 'What do you think it feels like to stand alone when everyone else has given up?' Allow 2–3 brief responses.
3. Tell the story of a young scout leader from a mountain village who, during a harsh winter, is the only one willing to guide a lost group of younger scouts back to camp through a snowstorm — comparing their steadfastness to a lone cedar tree on a ridge.
4. Pause at a dramatic moment in the story and ask the group: 'What would you do if you were that leader?' Invite 2–3 members to share their thoughts.
5. Continue and conclude the story, emphasizing the leader's calm decision-making and selflessness.
6. Close with a reflection question: 'What is one quality of that leader you want to practice this week?' Ask each member to silently think of their answer before the session ends.
materials: 1 printed or handwritten story script (optional, for leader reference)
1 candle or battery-powered lantern (for atmosphere, if indoors)
Seating cushions or mats — 1 per member

activity_name: Bread, Salt, and a Stranger's Door
objective: Members will understand the Lebanese cultural value of hospitality (diyafa) as an expression of community spirit and scouting's duty to others.
instructions:
1. Seat members in a semicircle and introduce the activity by saying: 'Today I am going to tell you a story about one of Lebanon's oldest traditions — opening your door to a stranger.'
2. Narrate a story about a Lebanese family in a mountain village during a difficult season who shelters a wandering traveler, sharing their last loaf of bread and salt despite their own hardship.
3. After the story, place a small piece of bread and a pinch of salt in the center of the circle as a symbolic prop and ask: 'Why do you think sharing was so important to this family?'
4. Invite members to share similar stories from their own families or neighborhoods where someone helped a stranger or neighbor.
5. Facilitate a brief group discussion: 'How can our scout unit act as that family in our own community this month?'
6. Summarize by connecting the story's values to the scout law of brotherhood/sisterhood and service, and challenge each member to perform one act of community hospitality before the next meeting.
materials: 1 small loaf of Lebanese bread (khobz) or equivalent
1 story script (handwritten or printed, for leader reference)
Seating mats or chairs — 1 per member

activity_name: The Scout Who Gave Away His Compass
objective: Members will explore the meaning of personal sacrifice by engaging with a narrative about a scout who surrenders his most prized tool to help a fellow member complete a wilderness journey.
instructions:
1. Open the session by holding up a real compass and asking: 'If this were the only compass on a mountain trail and someone else needed it more than you, would you give it away?'
2. Allow 2–3 members to briefly respond, then say: 'Let me tell you about a scout who faced exactly that choice.'
3. Tell the story of a pioneer scout on a multi-day hike in the Lebanese mountains who discovers a younger, lost scout from another troop and gives away his only compass so that scout can find the trail home, while he navigates by stars and landmarks alone.
4. Pause mid-story and ask the group: 'What feelings do you think the scout had when he handed over his compass?' Collect responses.
5. Complete the story, ending with both scouts reuniting safely at camp and the younger scout returning the compass with a handmade thank-you token.
6. Lead a closing reflection: 'What is something valuable — not just an object, but time, energy, or a skill — that you could give to help someone in your patrol?' Allow quiet thinking time before concluding.
materials: 1 printed or handwritten story script (for leader reference)
Seating mats or chairs — 1 per member

activity_name: Voices from the Ruins: A Beirut Story
objective: Members will connect with Lebanese collective identity and urban resilience by listening to and discussing a fictional first-person story set in post-crisis Beirut.
instructions:
1. Dim the lights slightly if indoors and ask members to sit in silence for 10 seconds before beginning, to set a reflective atmosphere.
2. Introduce the story: 'I am going to read you a story told in the voice of a young Lebanese teenager who woke up one morning to find their neighborhood changed forever — and chose to help rebuild it.'
3. Read or narrate a first-person fictional account of a Beirut youth who, after witnessing destruction in their neighborhood, organizes other young people to clean rubble, collect supplies, and restore a community mural on a damaged wall.
4. After the story, ask: 'What part of this story felt most real to you, and why?' Allow open discussion for 3–4 minutes.
5. Ask members to identify one moment in the story where the narrator showed a specific scout value (e.g., duty, courage, brotherhood) and explain their choice.
6. Close by inviting each member to complete the sentence: 'My identity as a Lebanese scout means I will...' — going around the circle with brief answers.
materials: 1 battery-powered lantern or dim lamp (for atmosphere)
Seating chairs or floor mats — 1 per member
Optional: 1 printed photograph of a Beirut neighborhood mural for visual context

activity_name: The Two Patrols of Qornet el-Sawda
objective: Members will be inspired to embrace outdoor adventure and collaborative problem-solving through a story about two rival scout patrols who must cooperate to summit Lebanon's highest peak.
instructions:
1. Gather members seated outdoors in a natural setting if possible; if unavailable, use a room with a projected or drawn image of Qornet el-Sawda on the board.
2. Begin by asking: 'Has anyone ever been on a hike where things did not go as planned? What happened?' Take 2 responses.
3. Tell the story of two scout patrols — one from Beirut and one from the Bekaa — who are competing to be first to reach the summit of Qornet el-Sawda. Midway, a sudden fog rolls in and one patrol member is injured. The two patrols must set aside rivalry and work together to carry the injured member and navigate safely down.
4. At the moment of crisis in the story, stop and ask: 'What skills should a scout have ready at this moment?' Allow members to call out answers (first aid, navigation, communication).
5. Resume and finish the story, highlighting specific leadership decisions made by members of both patrols.
6. Debrief with the question: 'When was the last time your patrol had to work with people outside your group to solve a problem? How did it feel?' Allow a brief open discussion before closing.
materials: 1 printed or laminated image of Qornet el-Sawda (A4 size)
1 story script (handwritten or printed, for leader reference)
Ground sheets or mats for seating outdoors — 1 per member

objective: Members will strengthen their sense of cultural identity and community roots by listening to a story about a grandmother who passes down the living memory of her village through a hand-drawn map.
instructions:
1. Before the session, prepare a rough hand-drawn map of a fictional Lebanese village on a large sheet of paper, marking landmarks such as the church/mosque, the spring, the cedar grove, the old souk, and the scout hall.
2. Unroll the map in the center of the circle as members gather around it, creating curiosity before a word is spoken.
3. Begin the story: 'This map was drawn by a grandmother who had never left her village. But inside it is the entire history of her community.' Narrate the story of a young scout who visits an elderly woman to interview her for a school project and receives the map as a gift — along with the stories behind each landmark.
4. As you describe each landmark in the story, point to its corresponding location on the hand-drawn map to engage visual learners.
5. After the story, ask members: 'If you drew a map of your own neighborhood, what landmarks would you include and why?' Allow 3–4 members to share.
6. Close the session by suggesting a take-home challenge: ask members to sketch a small map of their own street or village before the next meeting and bring it to share.
materials: 1 large sheet of craft paper (A2 or larger)
Colored markers — set of at least 6
1 story script (handwritten or printed, for leader reference)
Masking tape (to secure map to the floor if needed)
Seating mats or chairs — 1 per member

activity_name: The Scout Who Refused the Easy Path
objective: Members will understand that true leadership sometimes requires choosing integrity over personal comfort, through a story about a scout who must make an unpopular but honest decision.
instructions:
1. Seat members in a circle and open with a brief question: 'Has anyone ever had to tell the truth even when it was hard? What happened?' Accept 1–2 short answers.
2. Introduce the story: 'I want to tell you about a scout leader who was given two choices — and only one was right, even though it cost her everything she had worked for.'
3. Narrate the story of a patrol leader preparing for a regional scouting competition who discovers that part of her team's winning craft project was made by parents, not the scouts themselves. She must decide whether to report it to the judges and lose their ranking, or stay silent and keep the prize.
4. Pause before the decision point and ask the group: 'What would you do? Take 30 seconds to think.' Then invite a show of hands: 'Who would report it?' and 'Who would stay silent?' Briefly discuss the reasons behind each choice.
5. Continue the story: the scout leader chooses honesty, the patrol loses their rank, but the other scouts in the unit rally around her and she is later elected unit leader by her peers.
6. Facilitate a short debrief: 'What did the leader sacrifice? What did she gain? Can you think of a situation in your own life where you might face a similar choice?' End with a moment of quiet reflection.
materials: Optional: 1 small trophy or ribbon prop to hold during storytelling for dramatic effect
Seating mats or chairs — 1 per member

activity_name: Fire, Flute, and the Forgotten Feast
objective: Members will appreciate the power of communal celebration and shared tradition through a Lebanese folk-inspired story about a village feast that is nearly lost and then saved by the collective effort of its young people.
instructions:
1. Begin the session by asking members: 'Does your family or village have a celebration or tradition that you look forward to every year? What makes it special?' Take 2–3 quick responses.
2. Say: 'Let me take you back to a village in the Lebanese mountains, where a very old feast was about to disappear forever.' Begin narrating a story about a village whose annual spring feast — complete with dabke dancing, a bonfire, and a communal meal — is at risk of being cancelled because the elders are too old to organize it and the adults are too busy.
3. In the story, a group of young scouts steps up: one learns to play the flute for the dabke, another coordinates the food preparation, a third gathers firewood and builds the bonfire, and together they bring the feast back to life.
4. Midway through, pause and ask: 'What skill or task in this story could you personally take on in real life?' Allow 3–4 brief responses.
5. Finish the story with the whole village united around the fire, the elders moved to tears, and the scouts receiving the village elder's wooden walking stick as a symbolic gift of trust and continuity.
6. Close the session by asking: 'What tradition in your community could you help preserve or revive? How could your scout patrol make it happen?' Allow open responses and note any practical ideas for future service projects.
materials: 1 small wooden flute or recorder (optional prop for visual engagement)
1 battery-powered or real candle lantern (to simulate firelight atmosphere)
Seating mats or chairs — 1 per member

objective: Members will collaboratively design and build a freestanding bridge using limited materials that can support a weighted object, practicing teamwork and creative problem-solving.
instructions:
1. Divide participants into teams of 4–5 scouts each and distribute identical sets of materials to every team.
2. Announce the challenge: build a bridge spanning a 30 cm gap between two stacks of books that can hold a 500 g weight for at least 10 seconds.
3. Allow a 3-minute silent planning phase where team members sketch their design on paper before touching materials.
4. Give teams 10 minutes to construct their bridge using only the provided materials; the leader circulates and observes communication dynamics without interfering.
5. Each team tests their bridge by placing the weighted bag on top while the rest of the group counts aloud to ten.
6. After testing, hold a 3-minute group reflection asking: which team roles emerged naturally, what would you change, and how did you resolve disagreements during building?
materials: 1 roll of masking tape per team
2 sheets of A4 cardboard per team
2 stacks of hardcover books (to create the gap)
1 small sandbag or weighted bag (500 g) for testing
1 pencil and paper per team for sketching

objective: Teams will navigate a rope grid obstacle without touching the ropes, simulating an urban rescue scenario that builds strategic thinking and coordinated leadership.
instructions:
1. Before the session, set up a rope grid between trees or poles approximately 2 m × 2 m with 8–10 gaps of varying sizes at different heights to represent a collapsed structure.
2. Brief the teams: one scout is the 'trapped survivor' on one side of the grid and the team must pass every member through the grid gaps without touching any rope, within 15 minutes.
3. Each gap in the grid can only be used once, so teams must plan which member goes through which opening based on size and agility.
4. If any scout touches a rope, that gap is sealed and the team must find an alternative route; the leader marks sealed gaps with a ribbon.
5. The team designates a rotating 'commander' every 3 minutes whose instructions must be followed by all members; leadership transitions must be announced clearly.
6. After completion or time expiry, the leader facilitates a 3-minute debrief focusing on how leadership transitions affected team performance and which gap assignments required the most negotiation.
materials: 20 m of rope for grid construction
10 coloured ribbons for marking sealed gaps

objective: Scouts will solve a multi-part puzzle using only non-verbal communication, developing awareness of how body language and gestures convey meaning within a team.
instructions:
1. Arrange teams of 5 scouts seated in a circle; explain that from the moment the whistle blows, absolutely no speaking, whispering, or mouthing of words is permitted.
2. Distribute one envelope per team containing 15 puzzle pieces that together form a map of a fictional Lebanese village divided into five neighbourhoods.
3. Each scout receives exactly 3 puzzle pieces face-down and may not show their pieces to others — they can only hold pieces up or gesture to indicate where they believe pieces connect.
4. Teams must assemble the complete map non-verbally within 10 minutes; if any member speaks, the team must shuffle all pieces and restart the assembly.
5. Once the map is assembled, each scout silently points to the neighbourhood they believe represents their patrol's home base; the team must reach unanimous silent agreement by all pointing to the same area simultaneously.
6. Open the floor for a 3-minute verbal debrief: which gestures were most effective, how did it feel to be misunderstood, and how does this relate to listening skills in real scouting situations?
materials: 1 pre-cut 15-piece puzzle per team (printed village map on A3 cardstock)
5 envelopes per team for piece distribution

objective: Scouts will work as a coordinated team to transport fragile cargo across a market-themed obstacle course using a shared stretcher, building physical coordination and verbal communication skills.
instructions:
1. Set up a 15-metre obstacle course outdoors with 4 stations representing a Lebanese souq: a narrow alley (two parallel ropes 50 cm apart), a stepping stone path (flat stones or markers), a low tunnel (two chairs with a rope stretched between), and a final delivery table.
2. Give each team of 5 a stretcher made of two poles and a sheet of fabric; place a tray of 5 paper cups filled with water on the stretcher representing fragile merchandise.
3. All five team members must keep at least one hand on the stretcher poles at all times while navigating the course; a designated 'navigator' at the front calls out directions and warnings.
4. For every cup that tips over and spills, the team must return to the start of the current station and reattempt it; empty cups that survive to the end still count as successes.
5. Teams rotate the 'navigator' role at each station so that a different scout leads each leg of the journey.
6. After all teams complete the course, tally surviving cups and hold a discussion on how coordination and clear verbal cues impacted success rates across different navigator styles.
materials: 4 bamboo poles (1.5 m each) for 2 stretchers
2 sturdy fabric sheets (1 m × 0.5 m) for stretchers
2 lengths of rope (5 m each) for alley markers

objective: Scouts will build physical and emotional trust through a structured progressive trust-fall sequence, culminating in a full team catch that requires coordinated communication and mutual responsibility.
instructions:
1. Begin with a 2-minute safety briefing: explain proper catching stance (feet shoulder-width apart, hands up at chest height, knees slightly bent), establish a verbal safety protocol where the faller says 'falling?' and catchers respond 'fall away!' before any fall occurs.
2. Start with partner trust leans in pairs — each scout leans back slowly into their partner's hands at a small angle, practising the verbal call-and-response; run this for 3 minutes.
3. Progress to groups of three where one scout falls backward into two catchers positioned side by side; each scout takes a turn as the faller for 4 minutes.
4. Form the full team into two parallel lines of 4 facing each other with arms extended and interlocked in a 'cradle' formation; the remaining scout stands at one end as the faller.
5. Each scout takes a turn as the faller; after each fall, the team gives one word of feedback on what they did well as catchers.
6. Close with a seated circle reflection of 3 minutes: ask scouts to share one moment they felt genuinely supported and connect this feeling to how trust functions within a patrol during real camp situations.
materials: 1 soft grass or matted outdoor area (minimum 4 m × 4 m)
2 foam mats as safety buffers at the catching zone edges
1 printed safety protocol card per team

objective: Teams will encode and transmit a short message using improvised Morse code signals across a distance, then decode the received message accurately to complete a mission briefing.
instructions:
1. Divide scouts into teams of 6; within each team, split into two groups of 3 — a 'transmission unit' and a 'reception unit' — positioned 20 metres apart with no verbal contact allowed.
2. Give the transmission unit a mission briefing card with a 6-word message (e.g., 'meet at cedar hill at noon') and a simplified Morse code reference chart.
3. Transmission units use a torch or a flag (one flash/wave = dot, two flashes/waves = dash) to signal the message letter by letter to their reception unit.
4. Reception units record the signals on their worksheet, decode using their own reference chart, and write down the interpreted message.
5. Once the reception unit believes they have the full message, they wave a green flag to signal completion; the leader then compares their decoded message to the original card.
6. Teams score points for each correctly decoded word; if fewer than 4 words are correct, the transmission unit may re-send the failed portion once.
7. Conclude with a 2-minute debrief on which letters caused the most confusion and how real scouts historically relied on signal communication in Lebanon's mountain terrain.
materials: 2 torches or signal flags per team
2 simplified Morse code reference charts per team
2 clipboards with decoding worksheets per team
1 green signal flag per reception unit

objective: Scouts will untangle a human knot without releasing hands, practising spatial reasoning, patient verbal communication, and collaborative leadership under mild time pressure.
instructions:
1. Form groups of 8–10 scouts standing in a tight circle; each scout extends both hands into the centre and grasps the hands of two different people who are not standing directly beside them.
2. Without releasing any hands, the group must communicate and manoeuvre to untangle themselves into a single complete circle or two interlocked circles.
3. Introduce a variant rule for Pioneers and Rovers: one scout is designated the 'silent navigator' who can observe and direct but may not touch anyone or be part of the knot.
4. If a handgrip is accidentally broken, that pair must perform a 10-second freeze (hands raised) before reconnecting, giving other members time to reassess the knot.
5. Set a time limit of 8 minutes; if the group untangles within that time, challenge them to reform the knot and untangle again with a new silent navigator.
6. After the activity, ask the group: how did the navigator's perspective differ from those inside the knot, and what does this teach us about leadership positions in a patrol?
materials: 1 role card labelled 'Silent Navigator' per group

objective: Teams will prioritise, allocate, and physically distribute a set of emergency supplies across a simulated displaced camp in the correct sequence, demonstrating leadership decision-making and teamwork under realistic conditions.
instructions:
1. Set up 5 'camp zones' marked by coloured flags in a 20-metre spread; each zone has a sign indicating a different need: 'medical', 'food and water', 'shelter', 'children', and 'communications'.
2. Place a central supply depot with 20 mixed items (bandages, water bottles, tarpaulins, canned food, rope, batteries, blankets, etc.) in one pile at the centre.
3. Each team of 5 elects a 'camp coordinator' who reads a situation card describing which zones need which supplies and in what priority order, but may not physically carry items.
4. The coordinator has 2 minutes to brief the team before the race begins; during the race, the coordinator may only give verbal instructions from a fixed position 3 metres from the depot.
5. Team members transport items to the correct zones; each zone's attendant (a leader or older scout) checks items against a hidden checklist and accepts or rejects deliveries.
6. After 10 minutes, the activity stops; teams score points per correctly delivered item and per correctly followed priority sequence, then debrief on what logistics decisions were hardest to make.
materials: 20 assorted supply items (4 bandage rolls, 4 water bottles, 3 tarpaulins, 3 cans of food, 2 ropes, 2 batteries, 2 blankets)

objective: Blindfolded scouts will navigate a rope obstacle course guided solely by the verbal instructions of their sighted teammates, building deep interpersonal trust and precise directional communication.
instructions:
1. Set up a winding rope web on the ground using 15 m of rope in a meandering path with 3 'penalty zones' (hoops lying flat) scattered along the route; the path leads from a start marker to an end marker 10 metres away.
2. Divide teams into groups of 4; one scout is blindfolded and stands at the start, two scouts serve as guides positioned at specific fixed points along the route (they may not move from their spots), and one scout is the 'observer' who records guidance quality.
3. Guides must direct the blindfolded scout using only compass-direction language ('three steps north', 'turn east 90 degrees', 'stop, step left') — no casual directional words like 'left' or 'right' are permitted.
4. If the blindfolded scout steps on the rope or into a penalty hoop, they must stop, remove the blindfold briefly to reorient, then replace it and continue from that spot.
5. Each team member takes a turn as the blindfolded scout; after each turn, the observer shares one specific observation about the guiding communication.
6. Debrief as a full group: how did it feel to be entirely dependent on another person's words, and what made some guides more trustworthy than others?
materials: 15 m of rope for course layout
2 coloured cones for start and end markers

objective: Teams will strategically bid on puzzle pieces using a fixed budget of tokens, then assemble their purchased pieces into a complete image, practising resource management, negotiation, and collective decision-making.
instructions:
1. Prepare 4 identical 20-piece jigsaw puzzles; mix all 80 pieces together and lay them face-down in numbered lots of 4 pieces each, creating 20 bidding lots.
2. Give each team of 5 scouts a pouch of 30 bidding tokens and a copy of the puzzle image so they know what they are trying to build.
3. Conduct an auction for each lot: the leader holds up pieces face-up for 10 seconds, then calls for bids; teams hold up fingers to indicate their bid and the highest bid wins the lot.
4. After 10 lots are auctioned, allow a 2-minute 'trading window' where teams may swap pieces or lots with each other using token payments or barter.
5. Auction the remaining 10 lots, followed by a final 2-minute trading window.
6. Teams then have 4 minutes to assemble all their pieces; the first team to complete their puzzle (or the team with the most pieces correctly placed at time) wins.
7. Debrief: which bidding decisions were easiest to agree on, how did the team handle disagreements about spending, and what leadership styles emerged during the auction?
materials: 4 printed copies of the puzzle image (one per team)
120 bidding tokens (30 per team, e.g., poker chips or cardboard discs)
1 bell or whistle for auction signals

objective: Scouts will guide a blindfolded partner through an obstacle field using only verbal cues, building trust and practising clear, concise communication in a high-stakes simulated environment.
instructions:
1. Scatter 25 objects (cones, ropes, hoops, bottles) randomly across a 6 m × 6 m marked area to represent the 'minefield'; create a clear start and end boundary.
2. Pair scouts up; one is blindfolded and stands at the start, the other is the 'guide' who stands outside the minefield boundary and may not enter the field at any point.
3. The blindfolded scout must cross the field from one side to the other without touching any obstacle; touching an obstacle means restarting from the nearest boundary edge.
4. Guides must use only verbal instructions ('take two small steps forward', 'stop', 'turn 45 degrees to your left', 'reach down slightly to step over'); physical gestures are not visible to the blindfolded partner and must not be relied upon.
5. After both partners have crossed once, reconfigure the obstacle layout and swap roles so the previous guide becomes the blindfolded crosser.
6. After all pairs complete both roles, gather the group and discuss: what words or phrases were most effective, how did the level of trust affect the blindfolded scout's pace, and how can this communication style be applied in a real patrol scenario?
materials: 25 mixed small obstacles (10 cones, 5 flat hoops, 5 short ropes, 5 empty plastic bottles)
4 boundary cones marking the 6 m × 6 m field

objective: Scouts will work as a physically connected chain to collect lettered cards in alphabetical order from across a field, building real-time communication, pacing coordination, and collective leadership.
instructions:
1. Scatter 26 cards labelled A through Z randomly across a 10-metre area; each card is placed face-down on a cone.
2. Each team of 5 must hold a continuous chain — every member grips the wrist or shoulder of the person in front; the chain cannot break at any point during the activity.
3. On the leader's whistle, each chain moves to collect cards in alphabetical order; only the front person may pick up a card, but the whole team must agree verbally before approaching each cone.
4. If the chain breaks, the team must return to the start line and restart from their last correctly collected card; the leader holds any collected cards until the team returns.
5. Teams may not collect a card out of sequence — if they arrive at a card and it is not the next letter they need, they must leave it and search elsewhere.
6. The first team to collect all 26 cards in correct sequence wins; if time runs out at 12 minutes, the team with the highest sequential streak wins.
7. Debrief in 3 minutes: how did the team decide who led direction changes, and what happened to communication speed when the chain changed direction quickly?
materials: Boundary markers for the 10-metre play area

objective: Members will demonstrate core wilderness survival skills by rotating through timed challenge stations as a coordinated team.
instructions:
1. Divide participants into teams of 4–5 scouts and explain that each team will rotate through four survival stations set up around the meeting area.
2. Station 1 – Fire Triangle: Teams arrange provided materials (wet cloth, dry bark, flint card) to correctly identify which combination can start a fire, explaining why.
3. Station 2 – Shelter Evaluation: Teams examine three small model shelters built from sticks and leaves and rank them from most to least effective for a cold Lebanese mountain night, giving one reason per ranking.
4. Station 3 – Signal Flag Semaphore: Using two small flags, teams encode and decode a three-word distress message using a provided semaphore chart.
5. Station 4 – Water Source Judgment: Teams are shown three labeled photos of water sources (river, puddle, spring) and must rank them by safety and describe a purification method for each.
6. A timekeeper signals every 4 minutes to rotate teams; a facilitator scores each station on accuracy and teamwork.
7. After all rotations, teams reconvene and the facilitator reviews the correct answers at each station, reinforcing key survival principles.
materials: Assorted dry bark and wet cloth scraps (1 set per station 1)
3 pre-built stick-and-leaf mini shelters for station 2
8 small semaphore flags (2 per team) for station 3
3 laminated water-source photo cards for station 4
1 scoring clipboard with rubric for facilitator
Pencils and scoring sheets (1 per team)

objective: Members will work in coordinated teams to collect, sort, and record litter in a designated outdoor area, practicing environmental stewardship and organized service.
instructions:
1. Brief all participants on Lebanese Law No. 444 regarding environmental protection and explain that today's challenge simulates a real community cleanup operation in a Lebanese valley or park area.
2. Divide scouts into teams of 4 and assign each team a clearly marked zone (use rope or cones) within the meeting ground or nearby outdoor area.
3. Distribute gloves, bags, and a sorting chart to each team; explain the four sorting categories: plastic, organic waste, metal/glass, and hazardous (batteries, chemicals).
4. On the signal, teams begin collecting all litter within their zone and placing items into the correctly labeled bags, with one member acting as recorder tallying each item on the tally sheet.
5. After 12 minutes, call time and have each team count their totals per category and calculate their team's 'environmental impact score' using the provided formula (plastic = 3 pts, hazardous = 5 pts, metal/glass = 2 pts, organic = 1 pt).
6. Teams present their findings in 60 seconds, stating the most common waste type found and one preventive action the community could take.
7. Facilitator leads a 2-minute debrief on Lebanon's waste management challenges and how scouts can advocate for change.
materials: Latex or rubber gloves (2 pairs per participant)
1 tally sheet and pencil per team
Rope or cones to mark zones (20 meters total)
1 printed environmental impact scoring formula card per team

objective: Members will collaboratively design and assemble a Lebanese-themed mosaic flag from recycled materials within a strict time limit, developing creative problem-solving and team coordination.
instructions:
1. Introduce the challenge: each team of 4–5 must recreate the Lebanese flag as a mosaic using only the recycled materials provided, within 15 minutes of creation time.
2. Distribute one large cardboard base (A2 size) and the shared materials bin to each team; no additional materials may be sourced.
3. Assign roles within each team: designer (sketches layout first in 2 minutes), cutters (2 members), assembler (1 member arranges pieces), and timekeeper/quality checker (monitors progress).
4. Teams begin constructing their mosaic, using glue sticks to fix torn paper, fabric scraps, and bottle caps onto the cardboard in the proportions of the Lebanese flag (red-white-red with cedar silhouette).
5. At the 15-minute mark, all teams must stop and place their completed or partial mosaic on display.
6. Teams conduct a gallery walk (90 seconds) to view all mosaics without touching them.
7. Facilitator scores each mosaic on three criteria (accuracy 1–5, creativity 1–5, teamwork observed 1–5) and announces results, celebrating effort and creative interpretation.
materials: Assorted colored paper scraps (1 mixed bag per team)
Fabric remnant pieces (1 small bag per team)
Plastic bottle caps assorted colors (30 per team)
1 printed Lebanese flag reference image per team

objective: Members will demonstrate personal discipline and team coordination by completing a structured physical and mental drill sequence with zero verbal communication errors.
instructions:
1. Explain the challenge: teams of 5 must complete a sequence of four drill tasks in the correct order, silently (no speaking allowed after the start signal), relying entirely on eye contact and hand signals.
2. Distribute the printed signal code card to each team and give them 3 minutes to study and memorize the 6 hand signals used in the challenge (e.g., fist = stop, open palm = go, two fingers = rotate).
3. Signal cards are collected; the challenge begins. Task 1 — Formation: Teams arrange themselves into a specific formation shown on a posted diagram using only memorized signals.
4. Task 2 — Object Transfer: Teams pass a filled water bottle down the line and back using only their non-dominant hand, without dropping it; each drop adds a 10-second penalty.
5. Task 3 — Sequence Recall: The team leader (designated beforehand) silently signals the correct order of four numbered cones; the team arranges them accordingly.
6. Task 4 — Synchronized Salute: All five members must perform the scout salute simultaneously on the leader's hand signal with no one moving early or late (facilitator judges alignment).
7. After completion, facilitator debriefs on how silence, trust, and discipline in scouting relate to real-life leadership and emergency scenarios in Lebanon.
materials: 1 printed hand signal code card per team (collected before challenge)
1 posted formation diagram (A3 printed, mounted on stand)

objective: Members will assemble correctly packed humanitarian aid packages for a simulated Lebanese community in need, practicing precision, speed, and empathetic service.
instructions:
1. Set the context: Lebanon has experienced repeated humanitarian crises; scouts play a vital role in relief response. Today, teams simulate an NGO packing operation under time pressure.
2. Brief teams of 4 on the packing checklist: each package must contain exactly 1 water purification tablet, 1 bandage roll, 1 energy bar wrapper (stand-in), 1 printed instruction card, and 1 small hygiene item — packed in the correct order as per the checklist.
3. Demonstrate one correctly packed package, pointing out common errors (wrong order, missing item, box not sealed properly).
4. On the start signal, teams begin assembling as many correctly packed packages as possible in 12 minutes from the supply table; one team member acts as quality checker and must approve each package before it is stacked.
5. If the quality checker approves an incorrect package and it is caught by the facilitator, the team loses 2 completed packages from their total.
6. At time, facilitator and assistant do a rapid quality audit of the top 3 packages from each team's stack.
7. Debrief: discuss real Lebanese scout service contributions (e.g., post-Beirut blast relief, displaced community support) and what values drive scouts to serve.
materials: Water purification tablet packets (10 per team, can be labeled placeholders)
Individually wrapped bandage rolls (10 per team)
Wrapped energy bar stand-ins (10 per team)
Small soap bars or hygiene item stand-ins (10 per team)

objective: Members will complete a sequential outdoor course requiring correct knot-tying and compass bearing tasks at each checkpoint, reinforcing survival readiness and team discipline under timed conditions.
instructions:
1. Set up four checkpoints in the outdoor area, each marked with a colored flag and a task card; checkpoints are 15–20 meters apart in a non-linear arrangement.
2. Brief teams of 4: they must navigate from checkpoint to checkpoint using only the starting compass bearing given at the beginning; at each checkpoint, they must complete the knot or task listed on the card before receiving the next bearing.
3. Checkpoint 1 – Bowline Knot: All team members tie a bowline knot; only when all four are correct (checked by a stationed junior leader) do they receive the next bearing.
4. Checkpoint 2 – Clove Hitch on Post: Team ties a clove hitch around a wooden post strong enough to hold tension (facilitator tests by pulling).
5. Checkpoint 3 – Figure-Eight Follow-Through: Team ties figure-eight follow-through onto a carabiner stand-in ring; must be completed within 90 seconds.
6. Checkpoint 4 – Reef Knot Speed Round: Each member ties a reef knot simultaneously; the clock stops when all four are complete and verified.
7. Teams return to start and report total time; facilitator discusses where each knot is used in a real Lebanese mountain survival or rescue scenario.
materials: Rope segments 60cm each (4 per team per checkpoint = 16 total)
Wooden post or sturdy stake (1 at checkpoint 2)
Carabiner or metal ring stand-in (1 at checkpoint 3)
Checkpoint verification sheet for stationed junior leaders

objective: Members will design and build the tallest freestanding tower using only recycled materials, applying creative engineering thinking and environmental awareness within a fixed time.
instructions:
1. Frame the challenge with a Lebanese environmental context: Lebanon produces thousands of tons of plastic and paper waste weekly; today teams engineer a solution using that waste creatively.
2. Divide into teams of 4–5; distribute identical recycled material kits to each team and explain the rules: tower must be freestanding (no wall or chair support), built only from provided materials, and must stand for 10 seconds unassisted after time is called.
3. Teams have 2 minutes of planning time (no touching materials) to sketch a design and assign roles: structural engineer, material manager, builder (x2), and stability tester.
4. Building phase: 13 minutes to construct the tower using tape, rubber bands, newspaper, cardboard tubes, and plastic bottles provided.
5. At the 13-minute mark, all hands off; facilitator calls 'release' and teams step back; the tower must remain standing for 10 seconds.
6. Facilitator measures the height of each freestanding tower with the measuring tape; if a tower falls, its height is recorded as zero.
7. Debrief: teams share one design decision they made and one thing they would change; facilitator connects creative reuse of materials to Lebanon's circular economy efforts and scout environmental responsibility.
materials: Cardboard toilet roll tubes (5 per team)
Flat cardboard pieces A4 size (4 per team)
Pencils and blank paper for sketching (2 sheets per team)

activity_name: Girls in Service: Neighborhood Needs Map Sprint
objective: Members will collaboratively map and prioritize service needs in a simulated Lebanese neighborhood scenario, developing community awareness and practical service planning skills.
instructions:
1. Introduce the scenario: a Lebanese neighborhood has submitted five service requests to the local scout troop — a litter-filled park, an elderly resident needing weekly groceries, a school missing supplies, a blocked drainage canal, and a family displaced by flooding needing household items.
2. Distribute the neighborhood map (printed A3), scenario cards describing each need, and the resource card showing available scout volunteer hours (20 hours total across the troop for the week).
3. Teams of 4–5 read each scenario card and assign an urgency score (1–5) and an effort score (1–5) independently, then compare scores and reach a team consensus within 5 minutes.
4. Using their scores, teams plot each need on a 2x2 priority matrix (high urgency/low effort = do first; low urgency/high effort = plan later) provided on the worksheet.
