5. Teams then allocate the 20 available volunteer hours across the five needs based on their matrix, writing a brief 1-sentence action plan for each selected priority on the planning sheet.
6. Each team presents their top two priorities and action plans in 90 seconds, explaining their reasoning.
7. Facilitator leads a 2-minute debrief connecting the exercise to how Lebanese scout units plan real community service projects and the value of thoughtful, sustainable service over reactive efforts.
materials: Printed A3 neighborhood map (1 per team)
1 resource card showing 20 volunteer hours (1 per team)
Planning sheet with action plan template (1 per team)
Whiteboard or flip chart for facilitator debrief summary

objective: Members will create a pressed-leaf art frame using cedar and local plant leaves to celebrate Lebanon's natural symbols.
instructions:
1. Distribute one pre-cut cardboard frame (A5 size) to each member and explain its purpose as a nature art display.
2. Show members a collection of pre-pressed cedar leaves and other local plant specimens and discuss why the cedar is Lebanon's national symbol.
3. Ask members to arrange their chosen leaves on the cardboard frame in a pattern of their choice before gluing.
4. Using a glue stick, members carefully affix each leaf to the frame, pressing firmly for adhesion.
5. Once leaves are glued, members use colored pencils or fine markers to add decorative borders or write their name and unit in Arabic or English.
6. Allow frames to dry flat and display them on a string line across the meeting room as a group gallery.
materials: String line and clothespins for display

objective: Members will weave a simple friendship bracelet using the colors of the Lebanese Scouts and exchange it with a patrol partner as a symbol of camaraderie.
instructions:
1. Gather members in pairs and explain the significance of friendship bracelets as tokens of trust and companionship in scouting.
2. Distribute three strands of embroidery thread (green, white, and red — Lebanese flag colors) each 40 cm long to every member.
3. Demonstrate how to tie a starting knot and perform a basic three-strand braid, moving slowly so all members can follow.
4. Members braid their bracelets while seated with their partner, helping each other hold the threads taut.
5. Once braided, members tie off the end and trim any excess thread with safety scissors.
6. Pairs exchange bracelets and tie them onto each other's wrists, reciting a short friendship pledge prepared by the leader.
7. Leaders facilitate a brief reflection asking members to share one quality they appreciate about their bracelet partner.
materials: Embroidery thread in green, white, and red (3 skeins each)

objective: Members will paint geometric dabke-inspired patterns on ceramic tiles to explore and celebrate Lebanese folk art traditions.
instructions:
1. Begin with a two-minute introduction to dabke as a traditional Lebanese folk dance and explain how its costumes and embroidery feature repeating geometric patterns.
2. Show members three printed example tiles with dabke-inspired geometric motifs and discuss the use of color and symmetry.
3. Distribute one blank ceramic tile and a palette of acrylic paints (red, green, white, black, gold) to each member.
4. Members use a pencil to lightly sketch their chosen geometric pattern on the tile before painting.
5. Using fine-tip brushes, members paint their pattern carefully, starting with the background color and adding detail layers.
6. While paint dries, leaders encourage members to share the story of their pattern and what element of Lebanese culture it represents to them.
7. Completed tiles are arranged together to form a collective mosaic display for the meeting room.
materials: Acrylic paint sets with red, green, white, black, and gold (10 sets)

objective: Members will collaboratively draw and label a hand-made illustrated map of their local community, identifying key landmarks and places of importance.
instructions:
1. Introduce the activity by asking members to name three places in their village or neighborhood that matter to them — a school, a church or mosque, a garden, or a gathering spot.
2. Divide members into groups of four and distribute one large sheet of kraft paper per group along with markers.
3. Each group lightly sketches the outline of their local area using pencil, agreeing on what to include and how to orient the map.
4. Members assign roles: two illustrators, one labeler (writing place names in Arabic), and one border decorator.
5. Groups add color using markers, filling in green spaces, roads, and buildings with distinct colors for clarity.
6. Each group adds a compass rose in one corner and a legend explaining the symbols used on their map.
7. Groups present their maps to the wider unit, pointing out three community landmarks and explaining why each is significant.
materials: Large kraft paper sheets (8 sheets, A2 size)
Reference photos of local landmarks (printed, 2 per group)

objective: Members will hand-roll seed bombs using clay and native Lebanese wildflower seeds to later plant in community green spaces.
instructions:
1. Explain to members what seed bombs are and how they are used to restore green spaces and support pollinators in Lebanese urban and rural environments.
2. Distribute a golf-ball-sized portion of air-dry clay to each member and instruct them to flatten it into a small disc in their palm.
3. Members scoop a small pinch (approximately half a teaspoon) of native wildflower seed mix and place it in the center of their clay disc.
4. Members carefully fold the edges of the clay up around the seeds and roll it gently between their palms to form a smooth ball, ensuring seeds are fully enclosed.
5. Each member makes a minimum of two seed bombs and places them on a labeled tray to dry.
6. While waiting, members write their name on a small flag toothpick and insert it into their seed bomb tray section to track ownership.
7. Leaders lead a discussion on where the group could plant their seed bombs as a community service project, noting specific local green spaces.
materials: Native Lebanese wildflower seed mix (200g total)
Wet wipes for hand cleaning (1 pack)

objective: Members will weave a traditional straw-style bookmark using paper strips and decorate it with a Lebanese motif to gift to a friend or family member.
instructions:
1. Introduce the craft by showing members examples of traditional Lebanese straw weaving (hasir) and explain its roots in Lebanese village craftsmanship.
2. Distribute five strips of pre-cut colored paper (2cm x 20cm) in alternating colors to each member.
3. Demonstrate the simple over-under weaving technique using two contrasting strips as a guide, going slowly for Beavers and Cubs.
4. Members weave their strips together following the demonstrated pattern to form a flat rectangular bookmark shape.
5. Once weaving is complete, members fold and glue the loose ends to secure the bookmark, pressing firmly for 30 seconds.
6. Members use fine-tip markers to draw a small cedar tree, Lebanese flag, or scout fleur-de-lis on the bottom section of their bookmark.
7. Members write a short dedication message on the back of the bookmark for the intended recipient and share who they will give it to.
materials: Pre-cut colored paper strips in assorted colors (10 strips per member)

objective: Members will assemble pinecone bird feeders using natural materials to hang in their local environment and support native bird populations.
instructions:
1. Begin with a brief discussion on native Lebanese birds and the importance of supporting wildlife, especially during dry summer months when food is scarce.
2. Distribute one large pinecone per member and instruct them to tie a 30 cm length of twine securely around the top cluster of the pinecone.
3. Using a craft stick, members spread a thick coat of peanut butter (or sunflower seed butter for allergy-safe groups) evenly over the pinecone's ridges.
4. Members roll or press their coated pinecone into a tray of mixed birdseed, pressing gently so seeds adhere to all surfaces.
5. Excess seeds are shaken off back into the tray and members inspect their feeder to ensure full coverage.
6. Members place their completed feeder on a wax paper square and attach a small identification tag with their name and unit written in both Arabic and English.
7. The group discusses and agrees on a suitable outdoor location — a tree branch, fence post, or garden — to hang the feeders as a unit community service activity.
materials: Peanut butter or sunflower seed butter (500g)

objective: Members will collaboratively design and create a patrol flag from recycled fabric, incorporating symbols that represent their patrol's shared identity and values.
instructions:
1. Gather each patrol (groups of 4-6) around a table and explain the tradition and significance of patrol flags in scouting identity and unit pride.
2. Distribute one piece of recycled white fabric (30cm x 40cm) per patrol along with fabric markers and reference sheets showing scouting symbols and Lebanese motifs.
3. Patrols hold a two-minute discussion to agree on a patrol name, a central symbol, and two supporting design elements that represent their shared values.
4. One member lightly sketches the agreed design in pencil on the fabric while others prepare the colors to be used.
5. All patrol members contribute to coloring the flag using fabric markers, each taking responsibility for a distinct section.
6. Patrols add a short patrol motto in Arabic or English along the bottom edge of the flag using a contrasting color.
7. Each patrol attaches their flag to a craft dowel rod using tape or glue and presents it to the full unit, explaining their chosen symbols and motto.
materials: Recycled white cotton fabric pieces 30cm x 40cm (1 per patrol)
Fabric markers in assorted colors (5 sets)
Strong tape or fabric glue (5 rolls/tubes)
Printed scouting symbol reference sheets (1 per patrol)

objective: Members will fill and sew a simple fabric sachet with dried Lebanese za'atar and herbs to gift to a community elder as an act of service and cultural appreciation.
instructions:
1. Introduce the activity by discussing the cultural significance of za'atar (wild thyme) in Lebanese cuisine, medicine, and daily life, and the tradition of giving herbs as gifts.
2. Distribute one pre-cut muslin sachet (approximately 8cm x 10cm with one open end) to each member and explain its construction.
3. Members spoon two tablespoons of a dried herb mix (za'atar, dried rose petals, and lavender) into their sachet using a small funnel or folded paper guide.
4. Once filled, members fold the open top edge down twice and use a large blunt needle with colored thread to sew three simple running stitches to seal the sachet.
5. Members tie off the thread with a double knot and trim excess with safety scissors.
6. Each member decorates the outside of the sachet using a fabric-safe stamp or marker, adding a small cedar motif or their patrol symbol.
7. Members write a short greeting card in Arabic to accompany the sachet and reflect on which community elder or neighbor they will give it to.
materials: Pre-cut muslin sachets with open end (30)
Paper funnels or folded guide sheets (10)

objective: Members will create an individual mosaic collage using torn magazine paper to visually represent one line of the Scout Promise and display it as a group mural.
instructions:
1. Read the Scout Promise aloud with the full unit and briefly discuss the meaning of each line, encouraging members to identify which line resonates most with them.
2. Each member selects one line of the Scout Promise and writes it at the top of their A4 card in large letters using a marker.
3. Distribute old magazines and newspapers and instruct members to tear (not cut) small irregular pieces of colored paper to use as mosaic tiles.
4. Members sketch a simple background image related to their chosen Promise line in pencil on the card — for example, a cedar tree for 'duty to my country' or hands shaking for friendship.
5. Using a glue stick, members cover their sketched image area by area with torn paper pieces, layering colors to create depth and texture in the mosaic style.
6. Once the image is complete, members add a decorative border using torn paper in Lebanese flag colors (red, white, green).
7. All completed collages are arranged side by side on a wall or pinboard to form a full Scout Promise mural, and the group reads the Promise together once more in front of the display.
materials: Old magazines and newspapers for tearing (15 magazines)

objective: Members will craft a cedar-themed wooden bookmark that celebrates Lebanese national identity and practice basic carving and decorating techniques.
instructions:
1. Distribute one pre-cut thin balsa wood strip (15cm x 3cm) to each member and explain that the cedar tree is Lebanon's national symbol and appears on the flag.
2. Using a pencil, guide members to lightly sketch a cedar tree silhouette or the word 'Lebanon' in Arabic calligraphy on their wood strip.
3. Instruct members to carefully use a craft stylus or blunt carving tool to etch the outline of their design into the wood surface.
4. Have members paint their bookmark using green and brown acrylic paint for the cedar tree and allow two minutes for partial drying.
5. Once paint is semi-dry, members punch a small hole at the top of the bookmark, thread a red-and-white string (matching the Lebanese flag colors) through it, and tie a knot.
6. Members share their finished bookmark with the group and say one sentence about what Lebanese identity means to them.
materials: 8 craft stylus or blunt carving tools
Red and white string (2 meters cut into 16 pieces)
16 sheets of scrap paper (for painting surface protection)

objective: Members will transform used tin cans into decorative lanterns, demonstrating how everyday waste can be repurposed into functional and beautiful objects.
instructions:
1. Begin with a brief 2-minute discussion on Lebanon's waste crisis and how upcycling reduces landfill pressure, connecting the craft to environmental responsibility.
2. Distribute one pre-cleaned tin can per member. Ensure all sharp edges have been safely filed or taped in advance by the leader.
3. Using a permanent marker, members draw a simple pattern on their can — options include geometric shapes, stars, or a cedar tree silhouette.
4. With a leader supervising closely, members use a hammer and nail to carefully punch holes along their drawn pattern, creating a light-filtering design.
5. Members wrap the exterior base of the can with twine or colored wire to add decoration and a hanging loop at the top.
6. Place a small battery-powered tea light inside each finished lantern and dim the room lights briefly so members can admire the effect of the light through the punched pattern.
7. Discuss as a group where these lanterns could be donated or used in a community service context.
materials: 16 pre-cleaned, edge-filed tin cans (one per member)
2 rolls of colored wire (green or red)
8 wooden blocks (to rest cans on while hammering)

objective: Members will create greeting cards using pre-pressed Lebanese wildflowers and leaves, learning to identify local flora while producing a culturally meaningful keepsake.
instructions:
1. Display a reference poster or printed sheet showing five common Lebanese wildflowers (e.g., anemone, cyclamen, chamomile, wild thyme, and poppy) and briefly name each one in both Arabic and English.
2. Distribute a folded cardstock card, a sheet of pre-pressed flowers/leaves (prepared by the leader in advance), and a glue stick to each member.
3. Instruct members to select two or three pressed plant specimens and arrange them on the front of their card before gluing, encouraging creative composition.
4. Once satisfied with the arrangement, members carefully apply the glue stick and press the plants firmly onto the card, then lay a piece of wax paper on top and press gently for one minute.
5. Using fine-tip markers, members write the Arabic name of one flower they used and a short phrase such as 'من طبيعة لبنان' (From Lebanon's nature) inside or beneath the design.
6. Members write a brief dedication inside the card addressed to a community elder, neighbor, or family member as a service gesture, ready to be delivered.
materials: 16 folded cardstock cards (A6 size, white or cream)
4 sheets of pre-pressed Lebanese wildflowers and leaves
2 printed reference sheets of Lebanese wildflowers with Arabic and English names

objective: Members will create a small mosaic tile representing the Lebanese flag using torn paper pieces, building pride in national identity through a traditional mosaic art form inspired by Lebanese Byzantine heritage.
instructions:
1. Open with a 2-minute introduction to the art of mosaic, noting its long history in Lebanon from the Byzantine era to present-day Lebanese artists, and show one example image if available.
2. Distribute one piece of cardboard (15cm x 10cm) to each member, pre-drawn with the outline of the Lebanese flag divided into its three sections.
3. Provide sheets of red, white, and green tissue paper or magazine pages, and instruct members to tear them into small irregular pieces roughly 1cm in size.
4. Members apply white craft glue to one section of the flag at a time and carefully place torn paper pieces within that section, fitting them like a mosaic without leaving large gaps.
5. For the cedar tree section, guide members to layer green pieces in the center to form the tree shape, using a reference image for proportion.
6. Once complete, members apply a thin layer of diluted glue (mod podge style) over the entire surface to seal and give a slight sheen to the finished mosaic.
7. Allow tiles to dry flat and display them together to form a collective wall of Lebanese flags in the meeting space.
materials: 16 cardboard pieces (15cm x 10cm, pre-drawn with flag outline)
1 printed reference image of the Lebanese flag

objective: Members will create biodegradable seed bombs from recycled paper pulp and native Lebanese plant seeds, which they will later scatter in designated green areas as a reforestation service act.
instructions:
1. Explain the concept of seed bombing as a guerrilla gardening technique used by environmental activists worldwide, and connect it to Lebanon's reforestation needs following historical deforestation and recent fires.
2. Distribute a handful of pre-soaked recycled newspaper pulp to each member on a plastic tray and add a pinch of native seeds (thyme, wild chamomile, or local wildflowers) per leader instruction.
3. Members mix the seeds thoroughly into their pulp portion using their hands, ensuring even seed distribution throughout the mixture.
4. Instruct members to squeeze out excess water firmly, then roll and shape the mixture into compact balls approximately 3–4cm in diameter, making two to three per member.
5. Place all finished seed bombs on a labeled drying tray and set aside. Members clean their hands and workstation.
6. As a patrol, members decide together on a suitable public green space, park, or hillside where they will scatter the bombs during the next outing, and note the location on a small planning card.
7. Each member labels a small card with the seed type used and the target planting location to attach to the drying tray.
materials: 500g pre-soaked recycled newspaper pulp (prepared by leader)
4 packets of native Lebanese wildflower or herb seeds (thyme, chamomile, or anemone)

objective: Members will use natural plant-based dyes and carved vegetable stamps to decorate a small fabric square inspired by traditional Lebanese textile patterns, exploring sustainable dyeing practices.
instructions:
1. Begin with a brief introduction to Lebanon's textile heritage, noting historical use of natural dyes from plants like pomegranate, indigo, and henna in traditional Lebanese embroidery and fabric arts.
2. Display pre-prepared natural dye pads: beet juice (red/pink), turmeric solution (yellow), and spinach water (green) in shallow dishes, and explain the plant source of each color.
3. Distribute one plain white cotton fabric square (20cm x 20cm) per member and show examples of traditional Lebanese geometric patterns for inspiration.
4. Members choose a pre-cut vegetable stamp (potato, celery base, or onion ring cut by the leader in advance) and press it firmly onto the dye pad of their chosen color.
5. Members stamp their fabric in a repeating or mirrored pattern, rotating the stamp or combining multiple stamps to create a geometric design across the fabric.
6. Allow stamps to partially set for 2 minutes, then members use a thin brush to add fine detail lines or outlines in a contrasting natural dye color.
7. Finished fabric squares are laid flat to dry and can later be used as a neckerchief accent patch, a small bag decoration, or donated as part of a cultural awareness display.
materials: 16 plain white cotton fabric squares (20cm x 20cm)
Beet juice dye (1 cup in shallow dish)
Turmeric solution (1 cup in shallow dish)
Spinach water dye (1 cup in shallow dish)
Pre-cut vegetable stamps: 8 potato halves, 8 celery bases, 8 onion rings
Printed reference sheet of Lebanese geometric patterns (4 copies)
Drying rack or flat newspaper sheets

objective: Members will weave a simple knotted friendship bracelet using scout colors and then gift it to a fellow scout or a designated community beneficiary, practicing the value of service through a handmade gesture.
instructions:
1. Introduce the activity by connecting the concept of friendship bracelets to the scouting value of brotherhood and sisterhood, and explain that each bracelet made today will be gifted — not kept — as a deliberate act of service and generosity.
2. Distribute two 60cm lengths of cord per member in scout colors (green, white, and red to reflect Lebanese scouting and national colors) and demonstrate how to secure the starting knot around a safety pin clipped to a fabric board.
3. Teach a simple 4-step square knot pattern: left over right, tuck under; right over left, tuck under. Practice together slowly as a group for the first three knots.
4. Members work independently to complete a bracelet of approximately 7–10cm of knotted pattern, finishing with a securing knot and trimming excess cord with scissors.
5. Each member writes a short message on a small tag (e.g., 'بالخدمة نبني' — We build through service) and attaches it to the bracelet loop.
6. Members pair up and ceremonially exchange their bracelets, or place them in a collection box if designated for delivery to a partner community group, elderly home, or younger cubs unit.
7. Close with a brief reflection: ask two or three members to share how it felt to make something specifically to give away rather than keep.
materials: Green cord, 60cm per member (2 lengths each, total ~2m per 16 members)
16 small fabric or foam boards (for pinning work)
32 small paper tags with pre-punched holes
Thin string for attaching tags (50cm pre-cut pieces x 16)

objective: Members will work in small groups to construct a miniature model of a traditional Lebanese mountain village using only recycled newspaper, tape, and natural elements, reflecting on sustainable building and cultural architecture.
instructions:
1. Open with a 2-minute visual reference moment: show 3–4 images of traditional Lebanese mountain villages featuring red-roofed stone houses, church steeples, and cedar trees, and ask members what makes these villages distinct.
2. Divide members into groups of 3–4 and distribute an equal share of materials to each group: newspaper sheets, masking tape, small sticks, and green sponge pieces.
3. Challenge each group to construct at least three buildings using rolled or folded newspaper, shaping walls and roofs. Demonstrate how to roll newspaper tightly into cylinders for pillars and fold it into flat panels for walls.
4. Groups add details: small sticks become trees, torn green sponge becomes shrubs or cedar canopy, and flat newspaper panels painted with markers simulate stone-colored walls and terracotta rooftops.
5. Each group designates a 'village square' space at the center of their model and places a small cedar tree (twig or twisted newspaper) as a central landmark, symbolizing national identity.
6. Groups add one sustainable feature to their village design (e.g., a solar panel drawn on a rooftop, a rainwater collection barrel made from a paper roll, or a recycling station drawn in the square) and explain their choice.
7. Each group presents their village in 60 seconds, naming it and describing one environmental or cultural feature they intentionally included.
materials: Newspaper sheets (approximately 10 per group, 4 groups = 40 sheets)
Green craft sponge cut into small pieces (1 pack)
Markers in brown, red, grey, and green (4 sets)
4 base boards (A3 cardboard or flat cardboard pieces)
Printed reference images of Lebanese village architecture (4 sets)

activity_name: Ya Watan Ya Arz: Cedar Pride Anthem
objective: Members will learn and perform a patriotic scouting anthem celebrating Lebanon's cedar heritage, building national pride and group unity.
instructions:
1. Gather all members in a circle and explain the meaning of the cedar as Lebanon's national symbol and its connection to scouting values.
2. Distribute printed lyric sheets with Arabic text and transliteration for the anthem 'Ya Watan Ya Arz' composed for this activity.
3. The leader sings or plays a recording of the full song once so members can hear the melody and rhythm before attempting to sing.
4. Break the song into two verses and teach each verse separately using call-and-response — leader sings one line, members echo it back.
5. Add simple hand gestures for key words: arms raised for 'watan' (homeland), arms crossed over chest for 'arz' (cedar), and hands joined for the chorus.
6. Combine both verses and perform the full song together twice, the second time with increasing volume to build energy and pride.
7. Close by asking two or three members to share one word that the song made them feel, reinforcing the emotional connection to the theme.
materials: Pre-recorded audio track of the anthem x1

activity_name: Jarat Al-Hayy: Songs of the Neighborhood
objective: Members will collaboratively compose and sing a short community-themed song using familiar Lebanese folk melody structures, deepening their sense of belonging to their local neighborhood.
instructions:
1. Begin by asking members what sounds, sights, and people they associate with their neighborhood — collect five to seven ideas on a whiteboard or large paper.
2. Introduce a simple, well-known Lebanese folk melody structure (such as the Dal'ouna rhythm) and clap the basic beat together so everyone is synchronized.
3. Using the collected neighborhood ideas, work together as a group to fit three or four of them into simple two-line verses following the melody's rhythm, with the leader scribing agreed lyrics.
4. Practice the composed verses using call-and-response: the leader sings the first line and members repeat, then all sing together.
5. Assign different patrols or small groups one verse each to 'own' and lead when performing.
6. Perform the full composed song from beginning to end as a united group, with each small group stepping forward slightly when their verse is sung.
7. Celebrate the creation by giving a round of applause and briefly discussing how the song reflects real community values.
materials: Whiteboard or large flip-chart paper x1
Printed blank lyric template sheets x20

objective: Members will create and perform a layered nature-inspired song incorporating sounds of Lebanon's landscape, fostering environmental awareness and appreciation of the natural world.
instructions:
1. Lead members outdoors and ask them to stand still for 30 seconds, listening carefully to all natural sounds around them — wind, birds, rustling leaves, water.
2. Ask members to name the sounds they heard and list them aloud, then discuss briefly how Lebanon's natural landscape — mountains, rivers, forests — produces these sounds.
3. Divide members into three groups: Group A provides a rhythmic beat by clapping or patting knees to mimic rain; Group B hums a low continuous tone for wind; Group C adds a melodic nature-themed line sung in Arabic.
4. The leader teaches Group C a simple four-line nature melody with lyrics referencing Lebanese mountains, cedar trees, and flowing rivers.
5. Layer the groups one at a time: start with Group A's rhythm, add Group B's hum, then bring in Group C's melody, building a full soundscape song.
6. Once all layers are combined, perform the full composition for 60 seconds, then gradually bring each layer out in reverse order until silence.
7. Reflect briefly on how music can help us feel connected to nature and why protecting Lebanon's natural environment matters.
materials: Printed lyric cards for Group C x10
Optional: small percussion instruments (tambourines, shakers) x5

activity_name: Ahlan Wa Sahlan: The Welcome Round
objective: Members will learn and perform a traditional Lebanese-style welcome round song that celebrates each scout's place in the group, reinforcing a strong sense of belonging and inclusion.
instructions:
1. Arrange all members in a large seated circle and explain that this activity introduces a 'round' — a song where groups start singing at different times to create harmony.
2. Teach the full melody of the welcome round song, which uses the Arabic greeting 'Ahlan wa Sahlan' as its core refrain, by singing it through twice with the whole group together.
3. Divide the circle into three equal sections labeled Group 1, Group 2, and Group 3, and explain they will each begin singing the same song but staggered by four beats.
4. Practice the stagger with spoken words only first — counting 'Ahlan, wa, Sahlan, marhaba' — before adding the sung melody back.
5. Perform the full round with all three groups, with the leader conducting entry points by pointing to each group when it is their turn to begin.
6. Once all groups are singing simultaneously, maintain the round for two full cycles, then the leader signals all groups to end on the same final note together.
7. Discuss with members how the overlapping voices represent how each member adds something unique yet harmonious to the group.
materials: Printed lyric and notation sheets x30
Bell or chime for entry cue x1

objective: Members will learn fragments of three traditional Lebanese folk songs and piece them together as a relay, understanding the importance of preserving cultural traditions across generations.
instructions:
1. Open with a brief two-minute discussion on what cultural heritage means and ask members if they know any songs their grandparents or parents sing at home or during celebrations.
2. Introduce three short fragments — four lines each — from three different traditional Lebanese folk song genres: a Dabke work song, a lullaby (halhaleh), and a harvest celebration verse.
3. Divide members into three patrol groups and assign each group one song fragment to learn; display the lyrics on a poster or printed card for their group.
4. Give each group two minutes to practice their fragment with the leader circulating and assisting with pronunciation and melody.
5. Conduct a 'heritage relay': Group 1 performs their fragment, Group 2 immediately follows with theirs, and Group 3 closes — creating one continuous medley performance.
6. Repeat the relay a second time, this time with all members joining in on each fragment as it is performed, regardless of which group 'owns' it.
7. Conclude by asking members why it is important to keep these songs alive and how they can share them with family members at home.
materials: Printed lyric cards per group x10 each (30 total)
Audio reference recordings on phone x1

activity_name: Sawt Al-Kashsha: The Scout Voice Warm-Up Parade
objective: Members will build vocal confidence and group energy by performing a call-and-response marching song while moving together as a unit, building esprit de corps and scouting pride.
instructions:
1. Assemble all members in marching formation — two columns — outdoors in a suitable open space and explain that today's song is performed while marching in unison.
2. Teach the call-and-response structure: the leader calls out a line about scouting values or identity and members respond with a unified refrain such as 'Nahnu Al-Kashsha!' (We are the Scouts!).
3. Practice the response refrain five times standing still until all members are confident and loud, encouraging members to project their voices with pride.
4. Begin the march at a steady pace and introduce the first call-and-response verse, maintaining the rhythm of the march so footsteps and singing are synchronized.
5. Progress through four verses covering themes of service, nature, teamwork, and Lebanon, with the leader improvising energy by raising volume or speeding the march tempo slightly.
6. Halt the group after the fourth verse and perform one final full-volume united chorus of the refrain while standing at attention.
7. Dismiss members from formation with a scout salute and brief affirmation of their vocal energy and group unity.
materials: Leader's call sheet with all verses x1

activity_name: Min Jabaluna: Mountain Echo Song Game
objective: Members will participate in an echo-based singing game inspired by Lebanon's mountain landscape, practicing breath control and musical memory while connecting to the natural environment.
instructions:
1. Gather members in a wide outdoor space and introduce the concept of mountain echoes — explain that in Lebanon's mountains, sounds bounce between peaks, and today the group will simulate this phenomenon through song.
2. Arrange members into two groups standing 10 to 15 meters apart, facing each other, representing two mountain peaks.
3. Teach both groups the same four-line song with simple Arabic lyrics referencing Lebanon's mountains, valleys, and sky; sing it together twice before splitting.
4. Establish the echo rule: Group 1 (the 'singers') performs one line of the song in full voice; Group 2 (the 'echo') immediately repeats the same line at half volume, mimicking an echo.
5. Progress through all four lines in echo format, then switch roles so Group 2 becomes the singers and Group 1 the echo.
6. For the final round, both groups sing all four lines simultaneously in full voice, symbolizing the mountain peaks joining in harmony.
7. Gather together and briefly discuss what the mountains of Lebanon mean to them personally and how nature inspires music and culture.
materials: Rope or cones to mark the two group positions x6

activity_name: Bayt Al-Kashsha: Our Scout Home Anthem
objective: Members will learn and perform an original anthem about their scout troop as a second home, reinforcing a deep sense of belonging and emotional connection to their scouting community.
instructions:
1. Begin with a brief conversation asking members what makes them feel at home in their scout group — collect three or four responses and acknowledge them warmly before transitioning to the activity.
2. Present the written anthem 'Bayt Al-Kashsha' on a large poster at the front of the room, with both Arabic lyrics and a simple melody notation for leaders.
3. Read through the lyrics together once without melody so members understand every word and its meaning, pausing to explain any unfamiliar vocabulary.
4. The leader demonstrates the melody by singing the first verse alone, then invites members to hum along during a second demonstration before they attempt to sing.
5. Teach the song in sections — verse one, then chorus, then verse two — using repetition until each section is comfortable, spending approximately 90 seconds per section.
6. Perform the complete anthem together twice: the first time seated and focused on accuracy, the second time standing with hands placed over hearts to emphasize the emotional message.
7. Invite any member who wishes to share why the scout group feels like home to them, closing the activity with warmth and reinforcement of belonging.
materials: Optional: acoustic guitar or keyboard x1

objective: Members will perform a rhythmic clap-song that progressively includes every member's contribution, symbolizing community solidarity and the idea that every voice matters.
instructions:
1. Arrange members in a seated circle and explain the structure of the activity: a clap-song that builds from one voice to many, symbolizing how community grows when people join together.
2. The leader begins alone — clapping a simple four-beat rhythm and singing the opening line 'Ana huna, wa anta huna' (I am here and you are here) — demonstrating how the song starts with an individual.
3. One by one, starting to the leader's left, each member joins in by adding their clap and their voice, with the group holding the rhythm and melody steady as each person enters.
4. Once every member has joined and the full circle is singing and clapping together, hold the complete performance for two full song cycles, enjoying the layered energy of the full group.
5. Introduce a dynamic variation: the leader signals with a raised hand for the group to reduce volume to a whisper, then signals again to bring it back to full voice — reinforcing listening and group awareness.
6. Perform one final cycle at full energy, ending with a synchronized stop where everyone silences on a single clap together.
7. Discuss briefly how the activity felt when only one or two people were singing versus when everyone had joined, drawing the parallel to real community values.
materials: No materials required — body percussion and voice only
Optional: leader's cue card with lyrics x1
Optional: small tambourine for leader pacing x1

activity_name: Mawsim Al-Arz: Seasons of the Cedar Song
objective: Members will perform a four-verse seasonal song about the Lebanese cedar tree across all four seasons, connecting national pride with environmental stewardship through musical storytelling.
instructions:
1. Introduce the activity by briefly explaining the cedar tree's significance in Lebanese history, culture, and the national flag, and frame the song as a year-long journey through the cedar's seasons.
2. Display all four verses on a poster or projected sheet, each verse representing one season — spring growth, summer shade, autumn color, and winter resilience — with simple Arabic lyrics.
3. Teach the recurring chorus first since it appears after every verse: 'Ya arz Lubnan, rumzuna wa omanuna' (O cedar of Lebanon, our symbol and our trust) — practice it five times until confident.
4. Introduce each seasonal verse one at a time: the leader sings it, the group echoes line by line, then the group attempts the verse independently before moving to the next.
5. Assign each of four groups one seasonal verse to 'own' — they will stand and step forward when performing their season's verse while the rest remain seated.
6. Perform the full four-verse song with chorus in sequence, with each group rising for their verse and all members joining for every chorus.
7. Close by asking members which season they feel most connected to and why, and how the cedar tree's ability to endure all seasons reflects scouting resilience.
materials: Printed four-verse lyric posters x1 (large format)
Optional: seasonal nature images displayed alongside x4
Bluetooth speaker with reference melody x1
